turns.text: That is entirely correct, Mr. Justice Fortas.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: But the statute provides that the lawyer is confined to the amount that is received before Mr. Hopkins or Mr. Hopkins et al. has by reason of past accruals.

turns.name: HAROLD H. GEARINGER
turns.text: That's entirely correct, Mr. Justice Fortas.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Not only that, then you have a 25% ceiling on that base and then you have the discretion of the Court to cut below that 25%.

turns.name: HAROLD H. GEARINGER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Is that the way it works?

turns.name: HAROLD H. GEARINGER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. And, in a practical sense, the Court does not in any case or in all cases automatically grant 25%, and he should not. He should not --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Whether this 25% or not, and whether it is prospective or whether it is retrospective in the percentages that he's entitled to get, why should a man in the case with the wife and three children have to be entitled to get 25% of all of the payments that had been made to the wife and children if all he has to do is ask him if they are the wife or are the children? Now, don't you think that Congress has a right to say that it shall be limited to that -- to just the claimant himself rather than the other, and don't you think in a case of this kind Congress intended to have a strict application so that these people would not be -- this kind of claimant should not be overborne by lawyer's contracts?

turns.name: HAROLD H. GEARINGER
turns.text: Your Honor, I'm trespassing upon my time, if the Court will allow any longer?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: You can answer the question.

turns.name: HAROLD H. GEARINGER
turns.text: I think that we have concerned ourselves and I apologize for concerning myself so much with the function of this organization and not to the interpretation of the statute. We submitted to the Court yesterday and, if the Court please, I could now lodge with the Court we reproduced numerous copies of Judge Coleman's opinion that was coming at point from this.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: We've heard that draft yesterday.

turns.name: HAROLD H. GEARINGER
turns.text: The --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: That --

turns.name: HAROLD H. GEARINGER
turns.text: -- position of -- yes, Your Honor?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: That was the unreported opinion --

turns.name: HAROLD H. GEARINGER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: That I asked.

turns.name: HAROLD H. GEARINGER
turns.text: On my happy conscience, Your Honor, if the Court would like to have them lodged with the clerk. The -- the simple and final position in this case of the Government on one hand, and the petitioner's on the other is that we say that it was the intention of the Congress not to divide and separate bachelors and fathers and mothers since the benefit went to the family unit, and there is no provision in the statute under consideration making such separation. And, in that connection, the petitioner here in this case relies and urges very respectfully of this Court the position and interpretation taken by Chief Judge Hainsworth in the Fourth Circuit as being the proper interpretation of the statute. Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: May I ask you just one more question.

turns.name: HAROLD H. GEARINGER
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Justice Black.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I haven't yet heard, maybe it's not in the record, it's not yet mentioned, has this man been given payment on the basis of the total permanent incapacity?

turns.name: HAROLD H. GEARINGER
turns.text: Mr. Justice Black, unlike workman's compensation by percentages of permanent disability or non-permanent disability, these -- the benefits provided by the Social Security Act are measured on a formula concerning the payments he has made toward the trust fund and his eligibility by age and earning capacity in the past. It's automatically computed. It depends not upon a --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: It depends on whether he's injured, doesn't it?

turns.name: HAROLD H. GEARINGER
turns.text: It depends on whether he is disabled.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, what disability was found in this case?

turns.name: HAROLD H. GEARINGER
turns.text: In this case, there is -- the Secretary found that he was totally disabled, and payment --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Totally disabled?

turns.name: HAROLD H. GEARINGER
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: With hernia?

turns.name: HAROLD H. GEARINGER
turns.text: Under -- and a number of other things, combined together. In the Secretary's determination, the administrative --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I haven't heard about the other things yet.

turns.name: HAROLD H. GEARINGER
turns.text: Well, we haven't touched upon those, Mr. Justice Black, because the Secretary has decided he was disabled and we have no dispute with that decision. The Court found he was disabled and we have no dispute with that, so that the solitary question here is upon what base does the district judge fix a percentage fee. That is the --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I agree with you. That's the only issue here.

turns.name: HAROLD H. GEARINGER
turns.text: That's the only issue, Mr. Justice.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: It would -- we've been going around pretty much.

turns.name: HAROLD H. GEARINGER
turns.text: Yes. Thank you, Your Honor.
